agent_reply: Implemented the icon rewrite.
The new user-selectable `ICON_*` list is now only: unknown, smartphone, laptop, tablet, home, cat, dog, bird, circle, square, triangle in [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:53).

`IconLookup::getSprite()` is now context-aware in [IconLookup.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/SpriteDraw/IconLookup.cpp:427), with the Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and Cloud mappings you specified, including overlay placement. I kept the legacy string aliases like `"phone"` mapping to `ICON_SMARTPHONE` so old JSON does not become useless.

`FlyGuiItem` now supports `sprite_desc_t` overlays and draws the overlay as a second PNG call in [FlyGui.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:485). `ScrollView` now uses context lookup for user-configurable entries, while built-in actions like pairing, scan, info, and NTP use hard-coded sprites instead of fake semantic icons.
Main screen bottom-row phone/laptop buttons now use the Bluetooth variants directly in [MainScreenView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp:54).

I also bumped the secure NVS config versions and added legacy icon migration so old stored values like old `ICON_WIFI` do not accidentally become one of the new user icons. During build, I hit the existing secure `cloud_item_t` mismatch and restored the cloud password field because the rest of the firmware still depends on it.
Verified:
- `git diff --check`
- `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` succeeded.
Implemented the icon rewrite.
The new user-selectable `ICON_*` list is now only: unknown, smartphone, laptop, tablet, home, cat, dog, bird, circle, square, triangle in [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:53).

`IconLookup::getSprite()` is now context-aware in [IconLookup.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/SpriteDraw/IconLookup.cpp:427), with the Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, and Cloud mappings you specified, including overlay placement. I kept the legacy string aliases like `"phone"` mapping to `ICON_SMARTPHONE` so old JSON does not become useless.

`FlyGuiItem` now supports `sprite_desc_t` overlays and draws the overlay as a second PNG call in [FlyGui.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:485). `ScrollView` now uses context lookup for user-configurable entries, while built-in actions like pairing, scan, info, and NTP use hard-coded sprites instead of fake semantic icons.
Main screen bottom-row phone/laptop buttons now use the Bluetooth variants directly in [MainScreenView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp:54).

I also bumped the secure NVS config versions and added legacy icon migration so old stored values like old `ICON_WIFI` do not accidentally become one of the new user icons. During build, I hit the existing secure `cloud_item_t` mismatch and restored the cloud password field because the rest of the firmware still depends on it.
Verified:
- `git diff --check`
- `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest` succeeded.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/Security/Aegis.h
## Active selection of the file:
setTestTempPassword
## My request for Codex:
we are reworking how security works, there are now two keys, filecrypt-key and network-key, I want the filecrypt key to have limited exposure.
first, fill in `kSaltNetwork` with "36 22 5c 86 c1 70 f2 c1 11 d3 d6 df 57 6e 28 93 71 4e 52 6c d8 43 b2 6b df ee 1f 12 c9 a3 e5 b2 "

then extend `Aegis::init` to load two keys into memory, `hasMasterKey` should not be renamed and reflect if both keys are available. But `getMasterKey` and `setMasterKey` and similar getters and setters now need to reflect the names `filecrypt` and `network`
`setTestTempMasterKey` also needs to be split into two and `setTestTempPassword` needs to create both keys
agent_reply: Implemented the two-key split.
What changed:
- Added `Aegis::kSaltNetwork` with your salt in [Aegis.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.h:19).
- `Aegis::init()` now loads both `filecrypt_key` and `network_key` from NVS.
- `hasMasterKey()` still exists, and now returns true only when both keys are available.
- Replaced master-key APIs with:
- `getFilecryptKey()` / `setFilecryptKey()`
- `getNetworkKey()` / `setNetworkKey()`
- `setTestTempFilecryptKey()` / `setTestTempNetworkKey()`
- `setTestTempPassword()` now derives both keys using separate salts.
- Audio recording encryption now uses only the filecrypt key.
- Web auth/config encryption/cloud upload auth now use the network key.
- Updated `test_sdcard()` to install deterministic test keys for both slots.
Also made `Aegis::init()` open NVS read-only and close the handle after loading; setters open write access only when actually saving keys.
Verified with:
```text
pio run -e thefly_bringuptest
Build passed. I restored `src/version.c` after PlatformIO regenerated it.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h
## Active selection of the file:
WebServer
## My request for Codex:
we need a session-security-state type of structure to hold web browser session security information
only one session is allowed, even having two browser windows open is not allowed and will simply cause stuff to fail
the handler for "/get_info" needs to
* randomly generate 32 bytes, called the session-challenge, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-challenge, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-client, and remember this
* using the network-key and session-response-from-client, perform hmacSha256, derive a session-response-from-server, and remember this
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called the session-salt-from-server, and remember this
* the response from "/get_info" needs to include: session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
on the client (javascript), a similar object needs to track the session security information
the javascript ajax call into "/get_info" needs to:
* randomly generate 16 bytes, called session-salt-from-client, remember this, during the call into "/get_info", this is included
upon receiving a reply from "/get_info", which includes session-challenge, session-response-from-server, and session-salt-from-server, all of this needs to be remembered
at this point, the user has not entered in a password, we do not have the key to do anything, we cannot generate any of the responses
at some point, the user will enter in a password. When this happens:
* derive the network-key from the password (a copy of `kSaltNetwork` needs to be included in the javascript file)
* derive the session-response-from-client (from the session-challenge and network-key)
* derive the session-response-from-server (from session-response-from-client and network-key)
* validate the computed-session-response-from-server against the received-session-response-from-server
* if invalid, then the password is wrong or the server is malfunctioning
* if valid, continue
* generate 16 bytes randomly as session-salt-from-client
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* derive session-key from the network-key and session-salt
* use ajax to request "/get_cfg", included in the request header are: session-response-from-client, session-salt-from-client
* internal nonce counter is reset to 0
* delete the entire `#login_panel` from the page, and blank out any variables that may contain the password. All keys are to be stored securely using WebCrypto if possible
The C++ backend needs to have "/get_cfg" modified to handle all of that
* validate session-response-from-client using network-key and session-challenge
* return error code if not validated
* if validated continue
* session-salt is the 32 byte concatenation of session-salt-from-server and session-salt-from-client
* compute the session-key with the network-key and session-salt
* construct the reply with the information as JSON but as a gigantic string
* encrypt the gigantic string using session-key
* send the encrypted reply
When we get the data from "/get_cfg"
* decrypt the data into JSON
* if not authentic, stop and warn the user.
* if authentic, continue
* fill the page elements with data from the JSON
delete `WebServer::authenticateRequest` and all usages of it, later when decryption is implemented, the decryption results will indicate if the request is authentic.
do tasks up to here so I have a chance to review, I'll issue another prompt for "/set_cfg", which would reuse a ton of what has been built up to this point
agent_reply: Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
What changed:
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.
Verification passed:
- `node --check html\script.js`
- `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`
- targeted `git diff --check`

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.
Implemented the session-security pass up through `/get_cfg`.
What changed:
- Added single-session state and nonce helpers in [WebServer.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.h:23).

- `/get_info` now creates the challenge, server salt, server response, resets nonce state, and returns those fields in JSON from [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:263).
- Replaced the old timestamp/hash auth with session-response validation in [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:453).

- `/get_cfg` now validates the client response, derives a session key, builds one JSON string, AES-GCM encrypts it as one blob, and returns `magic4|version1|ciphertext|tag16` from [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:1015).
- Removed all `WebServer::authenticateRequest` usages, including file upload and set_cfg request-level auth.

- Frontend now derives the network key, validates server response, derives/imports the session AES key, requests `/get_cfg`, decrypts it, and fills page fields in [script.js](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:797).
- Updated generated [web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h) from the JS changes.
Verification passed:
- `node --check html\script.js`
- `pio run -e thefly_bringuptest`
- targeted `git diff --check`

One important caveat: browser login depends on `crypto.subtle`. If the browser refuses WebCrypto on the device’s plain HTTP origin, the page will alert and stop at login. Also, when we do `/set_cfg`, we should give client-to-server encryption a separate nonce domain or derived subkey so AES-GCM never reuses nonce `0` with the same session key.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/Security/security-policies-specifications.md
## Active selection of the file:
The following items:
* filecrypt-key
* network-key
* network configuration binary
are hashed together. The user is shown a part of that hash on the LCD screen. If the code is not what the user remembers, the user can suspect the device has been tampered with.
## My request for Codex:
sorry for the interruption, I was in another window and stopped highlighting the thing I wanted you to see, let's try again

Add to `Aegis` a calculation for a tamper-evidence-code, as described by the text I am highlighting in the IDE. The hash can be just SHA1, have one call that returns the full result and another one that ends up being a uint32_t portion of it.

Also, there needs to be a cached hash of the network configuration, and "/set_cfg" needs to compute that hash again, to detect changes. Detecting a change should trigger the filecrypt-key to be regenerated.
agent_reply: Implemented.
- `Aegis` now has SHA1 support, cached network-config hashing, full tamper-evidence hash, and a `uint32_t` tamper-evidence code.
- The tamper digest is currently:
`SHA1(filecrypt-key || network-key || SHA1(network_cfg_t bytes))`
- `Aegis::generateFilecryptKey()` now generates a random 32-byte filecrypt key with `esp_fill_random()` and saves it to NVS.
- `WifiManager` refreshes the cached network-config hash on secured config load/save/default clear.
- `/set_cfg` now compares the previous and staged network config hashes, and regenerates the filecrypt key after a successful Wi-Fi config save when the network config changed.
Key spots:
- [Aegis.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.h:13)
- [Aegis.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Security/Aegis.cpp:246)
- [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:904)
- [WifiManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:424)
Verified with:
```powershell
pio run -e thefly_bringuptest
git diff --check -- lib\Security\Aegis.h lib\Security\Aegis.cpp lib\WifiManager\WebCfgHandlers.cpp lib\WifiManager\WifiManager.cpp
Both passed.

Small heads-up: the working tree also shows `inc/web_assets.h`, `src/version.c`, and `lib/Security/security-policies-specifications.md` dirty. I didn’t edit the doc; the build regenerated the asset/version files.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h
## My request for Codex:
I swapped `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` with `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL`

the document `lib\Security\security-levels.md` is up to date and reflects what I want. `lib\Security\security-policies-specifications.md` reflects the maximum security practices but `security-levels.md` has priority.
do a clean up of mistakes when I did the switching of the `#define`
also, get rid of any code that deals with `legacy` nvs storage, we do not need to care about that until development has reached some form of release
agent_reply: Cleaned up the `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` switch fallout and removed legacy NVS migration paths.
Main changes:
- Removed old `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` branches and old linked-list compatibility paths from Wi-Fi/Bluetooth config code.
- Removed Wi-Fi and Bluetooth legacy NVS migration handling.
- Fixed fixed-entry structs fallout: removed `next_node` usage and cloud `name` usage.
- Added level-aware behavior:
- Level 0: `/get_cfg` and `/set_cfg` use plain JSON, cloud upload auth uses per-cloud password.
- Level 1+: config transport stays session encrypted, cloud upload auth uses `network-key`.
- Level 2+: network config changes regenerate `filecrypt-key`.
- FTP only starts when `BUILD_FTP_SERVER` is set and `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL <= 0`.
- Boot now loads Wi-Fi/Bluetooth JSON only for level 0; higher levels load NVS directly.
- Cloud list labels now use URL instead of removed cloud name.
Touched the key areas:
- [WifiManager.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h)
- [WifiManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp)

- [WebServer.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp)

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/Security/enrollment-mechanism.md
## My request for Codex:
just to check we are still following my intented design and policy
for both the wifi passwords and cloud passwords
when transmitted to the frontend, as long as the SSID or URL exists, it is assumed that the password exists but it is simply kind of write-only in that transport
when written back to the backend, as long as the SSID or URL exists, if the password is submitted blank, the meaning is "do not change", it is never "make the password blank".
verify, fix if this is not what is happening now
agent_reply: Verified and fixed.

Wi-Fi was already mostly aligned: `/get_cfg` does not send Wi-Fi passwords, and blank submitted passwords preserve the stored password. I tightened it so changing an SSID can still preserve the same row’s existing password when the password field is blank.
Cloud was not aligned before. I changed it so cloud passwords are now write-only too:
- `/get_cfg` no longer sends cloud passwords, even at security level 0.
- The frontend always leaves cloud password fields blank after loading config.
- On save, the frontend only sends a cloud password if the user typed one.
- The backend treats a missing/blank cloud password as “preserve existing”, matching by URL first, then by row position.
- A genuinely new cloud destination at security level 0 still requires a password.
Changed:
- [WebCfgHandlers.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebCfgHandlers.cpp:144)
- [script.js](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/script.js:931)
- Regenerated [web_assets.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h)
Verification: I ran `pio run -e m5stack-core2`; it still fails on the existing config issue: `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL must be defined`. That blocker is unrelated to this password policy change.

Cloud was not aligned before. I changed it so cloud passwords are now write-only too:
- `/get_cfg` no longer sends cloud passwords, even at security level 0.
- The frontend always leaves cloud password fields blank after loading config.
- On save, the frontend only sends a cloud password if the user typed one.
- The backend treats a missing/blank cloud password as “preserve existing”, matching by URL first, then by row position.
- A genuinely new cloud destination at security level 0 still requires a password.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/Security/enrollment-mechanism.md
## My request for Codex:
I know you just deleted it, but we need a `WifiApModeView` that is a `FlyGuiView`. This will display Wi-Fi credentials while in soft AP mode.
Place `sprit_wifi_50` in the top left corner (but not covering the top bar). If this is the default super safe randomly generated soft AP, then also put `sprite_security_50` beside it.

The bottom right corner, also linked to `BtnC`, put in a clickable `FlyGuiItem` that switches between `sprit_eye_closed_100` and `sprit_eye_open_100` when touched. Open eye means the info on the screen is displayed, closed means the sensitive parts are displayed as "**********"
Below the Wi-Fi icon, there should be 5 lines of text, similar to below
SSID:
<actual SSID>
Password:
<password>
IP: <IP of the internal web server, probably 192.168.4.1>
The IP is not sensitive
Below the IP line, use the smallest font, show a line of text that cycles through a few displayed data items, cycling every 3 seconds, the items are:
* "page loads: n", number of times the "/get_info" request has been served since starting the AP
* "logins: n", number of times the "/get_cfg" request has been served since starting the AP, it is not displayed under security-level 0
* "saves: n", number of times the "/set_cfg" request (also the reset password and reset memory) has been served since starting the AP
* "errors: n", number of times there has been an apparent error, such as security-level mismatch error, or, some form of authentication error, those are the important ones
* "downloads: n", number of times a file has been downloaded, also track deletions and uploads with this number even though it doesn't make sense
`WifiManager` needs getters for these, and they need to be reset when the Wi-Fi starts, there may be request URL handlers missing but we'll implement those later

I am aware that I did not include some sort of stop button. The user must either click the physical reset button or power button to exit. So add a red line of text, small, as the next line, hinting so. I have no idea if it fits on the screen but do it so I can see.
agent_reply: Implemented the new `WifiApModeView`.
It now:
- Shows `sprit_wifi_50` at the top-left below the top bar.
- Shows `sprit_security_50` beside it for the generated secure AP.
- Shows SSID/password/IP, with SSID/password hidden as `**********` by default.
- Adds the bottom-right eye toggle using `sprit_eye_closed_100` / `sprit_eye_open_100`, touchable and also tied to `BtnC`.
- Cycles the small stats line every 3 seconds.
- Adds the red “reset or power off” exit hint.
Also wired:
- Added “Default Secure AP” to the Wi-Fi scroll list.
- Selecting it starts `WifiManager::startGeneratedSoftAp()` and shows the AP view.
- Selecting a configured AP starts that AP and shows the same view.
- Added `WifiManager` web counter getters and reset-on-AP-start behavior.
- Counted `/get_info`, `/get_cfg`, `/set_cfg`, reset password/memory, downloads/deletes/uploads, and notable errors.
Main files:

commit_message: code neatened, remove cloud features with a predefine

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/AudioFileRecorder/DiskStats.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES
## My request for Codex:
clean up bugs I've introduced when I wrote in `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES`
agent_reply: Cleaned up the `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES` fallout.
Main fixes:
- Fixed a real overflow in `WifiManager::sanitize_network_config()` when cloud is disabled: it was iterating cloud entries using the station count.
- Made cloud-disabled builds use `CLOUD_SERVER_CNT_ALLOWED = 0` while keeping a legal one-slot storage array.
- Kept `DiskStats::refreshRecordingUploadStats()` scanning local recordings even when cloud upload is disabled.
- Made cloud-history pruning a clean no-op `Done` result when cloud is disabled.
- Guarded cloud upload globals/includes/callbacks/views behind `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES`.
- Cleaned `WebCfgHandlers` so disabled cloud config returns/parses as `cloud_uploads:false` without awkward `#ifdef` expression bugs.
Verification:
- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed with cloud disabled.
- Temporarily enabled `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES`, rebuilt `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, and it passed too.
- Restored the switch to commented-out afterward, and restored generated `src/version.c`.
